# Transcrição: 2.4-3 Facebook Split Testing for Offers

- **Duração:** 07:36

## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hey, this is Deepash and this video is a two-part video actually to demonstrate the power of split testing within Facebook and also how I test offers with a small group before publishing them out for a big event. Now in this particular case, this is a test that I've done ahead of Black Friday. So for anyone that's e-commerce, Black Friday is one of the biggest sales days of the calendar year, if not the biggest for majority of e-com companies and brands. Now what I wanted to do, and you can see the date here is ahead of Black Friday, I wanted to know which offer would best resonate with our particular customers for this store. So what I've done is I've selected a custom audience of existing customers. So number one, these are people that know the brand. So I want to kind of know from them what they're most likely to be interested in as a re-offer now that they know the brand. And I'll explain why that is shortly. And number two, I then want to see kind of what kind of response I can get by split testing that in a single audience. So the split test feature in Facebook, it allows you to take a single audience or a single, it doesn't even have to be an audience, it helps you take an audience or a strategy as it was. So for example, manual bid versus auto, or you can take an audience and split that and gives you results back which are, which avoid any kind of overlap. So if you take an existing ad set, and this is not a split test ad set, and you split that two or three times, and you put one ad in each, there's going to be overlap. And what that means is one customer could end up seeing all three ads. So actually if you're doing a split test and say, I want to see which of ads one, two, or three works best, actually it could be that the customer saw all three ads and they all worked well together in a sequence. And then what happens is I see this often, people will do a split test this way, they'll then publish the ad that they think's working and all of a sudden it doesn't work. Well actually when you do it as a split test tool, and that's this little icon here, then what Facebook does is takes a single audience and splits it so that each split only sees that one ad. So there's no chance of crossover with this. So if I show you this here, I've actually taken a particular custom audience, and it's been split as many times as I've wanted, and in this case it's split three times. So if I just show you the inside of the particular split test, you can ignore this conversion thing, and this is actually an annoying thing of business manager, it's actually selected my personal profile here and not my business manager so I don't have access to this conversion event. So if you ever see that and you're certain that it should have the right data, just switch into the right business profile, I want to do that now because it'll just mess up my screen

## [03:02 - 06:05]

setup. So if I kind of go down here, you can see that I've set a lifetime budget, I've split the budget accordingly, and I have a segment of my email list here, and I have my location. So it's taken that email list, and it's created exactly the same ad set three times because I want to test three things here. So within each of these particular ad set, there is only one ad running. And what I've done is to take one audience, split it three times, and Facebook assigns the budget as I've asked it to over a three-way split, and then I have one offer running in each ad set. So I have one ad set which is running 10% off, and this is a store-wide offer. I have one ad set as a control which has no offer running, and then I have one that's promoting a discounted category, so for this one in particular it's jackets. So the goal of this test was to find out when it comes to Black Friday, which kind of offer should we run. There's another one I'll be testing shortly as well, it's a bundle offer, and we'll test a few more ahead of Black Friday as well. But I want to know which one actually gets most of the people interested. So for the sake of seeing what would happen with no offer, I've put a control in there which is just a general ad saying, you know, this is the brand, and it's one of our typical top of funnel ads, whereas for the jackets we're saying this jacket is discounted, I think it's 25%, on this one it's saying the whole store is discounted 10%. So you can have your assumptions and maybe say a 10% offer is going to be great, but in particular the jackets one worked best. Now this is Swedish corona, just in case you're wondering. Now there was a good row ass, and you can look at this and say, oh wow, this is amazing. Look, the purchase is just one. There is no statistical evidence here to say that one ad is better than the other one. So I'm not looking at cost per purchase, I'm not looking at rice. The spend on this particular test was really, really low. It was a 48 hour sell. We actually maxed out budgets and we didn't even spend it over this period because there just weren't enough people in that audience. But what I'm looking at is how many impressions did I actually get overall? I got the thousand impressions roughly that I wanted for each of the ads, ads that were running, the ad sets. And then what I'm looking at is how many people actually progressed through. So if you look at this, from a thousand impressions, a hundred went on to view the product page, two added to basket and three initiated checkout. And that's where the 10% offer, and you compare that to the jackets offer, and there's a huge, comparatively, there's a huge amount more people that actually went through the funnel, ultimately only one purchase, and this is actually only running for two days at the 48 hour sell. But the fact here is that gives me more confidence that the jackets offer is actually the offer

## [06:05 - 07:36]

that we need to lead with. So my cost per landing page view is super low. The cost per ad to basket is super low, initiate checkout, etc. So these might be fluke sales that we've got through. The next part of this is actually to put more budget through and get a bigger audience, actually test it with a cold audience. So we take a segment of our cold audience and then test it again there and see if they've got any valid results there. The reason for testing with existing customers for this particular store was actually the store owner was really against pushing promotions to a cold audience. So we wanted to prove things below the surface to existing customers. Actually, this gives me confidence that actually the jackets is the best offer. The 10% offer we may try and rejig that will continue running a no offer and will introduce a new bundles offer as well. But it's a great way of actually just seeing which kind of offer and creative is going to work best for your particular store as well. So with a few things to consider here is if you are in a position where you don't really want to test offers above the line to cold audiences, people that have never heard of your store before or clicked, then you can actually do it to existing customers. But the second thing is if you want to do proper scientific testing on a single audience and you want to test promotions or you want to test strategies, so manual bid, different manual bid levels, etc, etc, then actually using the split test tool is a cleaner way of doing that.
